coredata.dc:title: Microwave–hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from graphite oxide

coredata.dc:description: Graphite oxide (GO), candle soot, conductive carbon black and lampblack were used to prepare fluorescent carbon dots (CDots) by heating under reflux in nitric acid. The CDots prepared from GO exhibited the highest quantum yield and narrowest emission of those produced. Microwave-assisted techniques were also used to synthesize CDots. Compared with conventional heating under reflux, microwave-assisted heating under reflux and a microwave–hydrothermal method both shortened the reaction time. CDots prepared using microwave-assisted techniques exhibited increased absorption, higher quantum yield and longer fluorescence lifetime than those prepared by conventional heating under reflux.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDots by heating under reflux in nitric acid
Preparation of CDots by microwave-assisted heating under reflux
Preparation of CDots by microwave–hydrothermal synthesis
Comparison of CDots prepared using different routes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDots fabricated from GO (solid lines) or candle soot (dashed lines).
Optimization of reaction time for different synthetic routes to CDots: (A) heating under reflux in nitric acid; (B) microwave-assisted heating under reflux; (C) microwave–hydrothermal method.

HRTEM micrographs (a)–(c) and particle size distributions (d)–(f) of CDots prepared from GO by different routes: (a) and (d), heating under reflux in nitric acid; (b) and (e), microwave-assisted heating under reflux; (c) and (f), microwave–hydrothermal method.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDots prepared from GO by different routes: microwave–hydrothermal method (dotted line), microwave-assisted heating under reflux (dashed line) and heating under reflux in nitric acid (solid line).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDots prepared via different routes: heating under reflux in nitric acid (solid line); microwave-assisted heating under reflux (dashed line); microwave–hydrothermal method (dotted line).

Fluorescence lifetime of CDots prepared from GO by different routes: heating under reflux in nitric acid (solid line); microwave-assisted heating under reflux (dashed line); microwave–hydrothermal method (dotted line).
Quantum Yield and FWHM of CDots prepared from different precursors.
Quantum yields and lifetimes of CDots prepared from GO by different routes.

row.entry: Heating under reflux in nitric acid

head.ce:title: Microwave–hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from graphite oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphite oxide (GO), candle soot, conductive carbon black and lampblack were used to prepare fluorescent carbon dots (CDots) by heating under reflux in nitric acid. The CDots prepared from GO exhibited the highest quantum yield and narrowest emission of those produced. Microwave-assisted techniques were also used to synthesize CDots. Compared with conventional heating under reflux, microwave-assisted heating under reflux and a microwave–hydrothermal method both shortened the reaction time. CDots prepared using microwave-assisted techniques exhibited increased absorption, higher quantum yield and longer fluorescence lifetime than those prepared by conventional heating under reflux.

ce:section.ce:para: Various nanostructured carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes [1], graphene [2], nanofibers [3], fullerenes [4] and nanodiamonds [5] have been developed. Because of their potentially superior performance, these carbon nanomaterials have been applied in a broad range of fields [6]. Recently, a novel carbon nanomaterial, fluorescent carbon dots (CDots), has exhibited huge potential in applications including biological labeling, bioimaging and life sciences [7–12]. CDots possess excellent fluorescent properties compared with fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots, such as strong fluorescence, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, broad excitation spectra, narrow and tunable emission spectra [12]. In particular, brighter fluorescence (i.e., increased fluorescence quantum yield) is observed after passivation by covalent binding of organic groups [13,14]. In addition, CDots are biocompatible, small in size and low in molecular weight, and have low toxicity, making them superior to quantum dots [11,12].

Many precursors, including graphite [15–18], carbon nanotubes [19,20], an ammonium carboxylate salt [21,22], carbon soot [23,24] and carbohydrates [14,25] have been used to prepare CDots. In general, fluorescent CDots are successfully obtained by methods involving laser irradiation [13,18], thermal decomposition [21,22], electrochemistry [15–17,20] or heating under reflux in nitric acid [11,14,23,24]. Sun et al. [13] prepared highly luminescent CDots by a complex method combining laser ablation, heating under reflux in nitric acid and prolonged passivation. Thermal decomposition methods can directly produce functionalized CDots, but require a suitable carbon source [21,22]. Several research groups have reported various innovative routes for achieving CDots through electrochemical oxidation of graphite electrodes in buffer solution or ionic liquids [15–17]. The CDots consisting of multiple colors synthesized by oxidation of carbon soot in nitric acid consistently suffer from a low quantum yield [23]. More recently, it was found that microwave pyrolysis of glucose in polyethylene glycol can produce fluorescent CDots in just several minutes [25]. Although various precursors and methods have been developed for preparing CDots, unfortunately, most of the CDots produced display weak fluorescence. On the other hand, the fluorescence of CDots was enhanced after surface passivation with amino-terminated polymers, but the accrescent size of modified CDots confined its further applications. In general, the synthesis of fluorescent CDots to date has many drawbacks, such as long and complex processes, strict experimental conditions and producing CDots with low quantum yields. Rapid, convenient and efficient methods are still required for producing fluorescent CDots for various applications.

Microwave-assisted techniques have shown significant progress in their application to materials synthesis because of rapid heating and the resulting dramatic increase in reaction rate [26,27]. Moreover, there are many examples of nanoparticles being prepared by hydrothermal methods [28]. The combination of both routes is known as microwave–hydrothermal synthesis, which has recently been used to prepare nanomaterials [29–31].

In this paper, microwave-assisted heating under reflux and microwave–hydrothermal routes with graphite oxide (GO) as a precursor were used to prepare fluorescent CDots. The CDots attained via these routes exhibit higher quantum yields and absorption, and longer lifetimes than CDots prepared by heating under reflux in nitric acid. An added advantage is that microwave-assisted techniques are simple and rapid compared with heating under reflux in acid for extended periods.

Specpure graphite was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (China), candle soot was collected from smoldering candles, lamp black was purchased from Anhui Jixi Hukaiwen Ink Industry Co. (China), and fluorescein was purchased from Shanghai SSS Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Ultrapure water was prepared with a Milli-Q-Plus system (18.2MΩ) and used throughout the experiments. All other reagents were used as received.
GO was synthesized from specpure graphite powder according to the method of Hummers [32]. The final product was dehydrated and kept in a desiccator until required for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDots by heating under reflux in nitric acid
ce:section.ce:para: CDots were prepared from candle soot, lampblack or conductive carbon black according to literature procedures [23,33]. CDots from GO were synthesized using a similar route. Briefly, GO (0.2g) was added to HNO3 (5mol/L, 150mL) and then the mixture was heated under reflux at 140°C for 12h. The resulting dark brown solution was neutralized with sodium carbonate. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 15min using a Z383K centrifuge (HERMLE, Germany) and loaded into dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off=1000, Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China) and dialyzed against ultrapure water for 48h. The purified solution of CDots was maintained at 4°C until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDots by microwave-assisted heating under reflux
ce:section.ce:para: GO (0.2g) was added to HNO3 (5mol/L, 150mL), and then the mixture was heated under reflux at 120°C for 8h under microwave irradiation in a microwave reactor (WF-4000, PreeKem Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China) operating at a power of 900W. The obtained dark brown solution was neutralized and dialyzed as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDots by microwave–hydrothermal synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: GO (0.2g) and HNO3 (14mol/L, 6mL) were added into a Teflon microwave reaction vessel (Chengdu Guangpu Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The sealed vessel was then heated using microwave irradiation for 15–60min in a microwave reactor (Galanz WP800TL23-K3, Guangdong Galanz Group Co., Ltd., China) operating at a power of 800W. After the vessel had cooled to room temperature, the resulting light brown solution was neutralized and dialyzed as described above.

The size and dispersivity of the CDots were determined by a field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA). The size distribution was determined by measuring the dimensions of ca. 100 particles. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CDots were recorded on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an F-4500 fluorometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The quantum yield of the CDots was calculated by comparing the integrated photoluminescence intensities and absorption of the samples with those of fluorescein (literature quantum yield of 0.95). The fluorescence lifetime was measured on a Fluorolog-3 fluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc, France). An excitation wavelength of 460nm was used and emission was collected at 520nm.

Four kinds of carbon materials, candle soot, lampblack, conductive carbon black and GO were used to prepare fluorescent CDots by heating under reflux in nitric acid. Specpure graphite was also used as a precursor but unfortunately no CDots were obtained because graphite hardly reacted with nitric acid heated under reflux. What’s more, GO aqueous solution showed no fluorescence without reacting with nitric acid. The CDots yields from different carbon materials are low (GO CDots 0.75–1%, other CDots 1–1.25%) for the conventional refluxing. As shown in Fig. 1 , all of the CDots obtained from different carbon materials exhibited similar absorption and fluorescence spectra without any distinct absorption peak. The excitation and emission peak maxima are accordingly similar. These results indicate that all of the samples contain the same fluorochrome. The shapes of fluorescence spectra in Fig. 1 were broad and irregular, suggesting that some impurities in the solutions of CDots and influence its fluorescence. The GO CDots exhibited the highest quantum yield and narrowest full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the four samples (Table 1 and Fig. 1). Candle soot and lampblack may contain some impurities because they were collected from smoldering candles and burning tung oil, respectively. As an industrial raw material, conductive carbon black also contains some impurities. The impurities in these precursors may reduce the fluorescence of the resulting CDots. In addition, the GO CDots have the higher oxidation degree by oxidizing graphite. Accordingly, GO was chosen as the precursor to prepare CDots. The obtained CDots exhibited a high quantum yield and narrow emission because of the high purity and higher oxidation degree of GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of CDots prepared using different routes

ce:section.ce:para: CDots were fabricated from GO using three different routes: heating under reflux in nitric acid, microwave-assisted heating under reflux and a microwave–hydrothermal method. The reaction conditions were optimized and the fluorescent characteristics of the resulting CDots were compared (Fig. 2 ).

For the route involving heating under reflux in nitric acid, detectable fluorescence from CDots appeared after reaction for 8h, and the quantum yield reached its maximum after 12h of reaction. Meanwhile, fluorescence from CDots fabricated by microwave-assisted heating under reflux appeared after a reaction time of 4h, and the maximum quantum yield was achieved after reaction for 8h. For the microwave–hydrothermal method, the optimal reaction time was just 30–45 min; the quantum yield of the CDots was lower if a longer reaction time was used. Because of thermal and nonthermal effects during microwave irradiation, microwave-assisted heating under reflux reduced the reaction time from 12 to 8h. The microwave–hydrothermal method reduced the reaction time dramatically to just 30–45 min because it combines the advantages of both microwave-assisted heating and hydrothermal methods. In a sealed vessel, hydrothermal processes allow solvents to be raised to temperatures above their boiling points by increasing the pressure [28]. The reaction conditions used in this method lead to the formation of monodisperse nanoparticles. On the other hand, using the microwave–hydrothermal method with a prolonged reaction time may cause the CDots to decompose, resulting in a decreased quantum yield. The GO CDots yields by three routes were as much as above (conventional refluxing and microwave-assisted refluxing were 0.75–1%, microwave–hydrothermal was 1–2.5%). The position and FWHM of emission peaks of the CDots prepared by the same route did not change obviously as the reaction time increased, which indicates that the same fluorochrome was produced by each synthetic method.

Typical TEM images of the CDots produced from GO are shown in Fig. 3 . Fluorescent CDots with a uniform spherical shape are well dispersed and show the size distribution of diameters of 3.3±1.1nm, 3.8±1.4nm and 4.0±1.4nm for the heating under reflux in nitric acid, microwave-assisted heating under reflux and microwave–hydrothermal method, respectively. The average diameter of the fluorescent CDots is similar to that of other reported CDots [13,15,23]. It is inferred that GO decomposed into nanosized particles during each reaction. The CDots prepared by the microwave-assisted routes have a tendency to aggregate, which could explain the larger average diameter and size distribution (Fig. 3).

The absorption spectra of CDots prepared by the three different routes are compared in Fig. 4 . No appreciable absorption peak is observed for the CDots formed by heating under reflux in nitric acid because of strong background absorption. However, the CDots formed by both microwave-based methods exhibit a peak near 300nm. It is believed that the source of this absorption peak is a special structure in the CDots that is generated by microwave-assisted techniques.

The CDots produced using different methods possess similar fluorescence spectra. Excitation and emission wavelength maxima are near 470 and 520nm, as shown in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence spectra of the CDots prepared by different methods do not depend on the excitation wavelength. This differs from CDots synthesized by thermal decomposition or passivation [13,22] and reveals an important advantage of using GO over other precursors for preparing CDots. It may be due to the special structure of GO CDots.

Among the CDots produced using the three different methods, another marked finding is that the CDots generated from the microwave–hydrothermal method show the narrowest FWHM (70nm); the FWHM of the CDots produced by microwave-assisted heating under reflux and heating under reflux in nitric acid were about 90 and 100nm, respectively.

The quantum yield of the fluorescent CDots was calculated using fluorescein (excited at 460nm) as a reference. As shown in Table 2 , the quantum yield of the CDots prepared using the microwave–hydrothermal method is the highest of the CDots. The CDots formed by heating under reflux in nitric acid exhibit the lowest quantum yield, which is similar to those of other CDots reported previously [23,24].

The fluorescence lifetimes of the CDots fabricated by three different methods were measured by time-correlated single photon counting, with excitation and emission wavelengths of 460 and 520nm (Table 2 and Fig. 6 ). Of the CDots, those prepared by the microwave–hydrothermal method possessed the longest lifetime while those produced from heating under reflux in nitric acid showed the shortest lifetime.

Of the three techniques used, the microwave–hydrothermal method is the optimal route because the CDots obtained using this route exhibited the narrowest emission, longest fluorescence lifetime and highest quantum yield. In addition, this microwave-assisted method produces CDots in a significantly shorter time.

The excellent heating capacity of microwave irradiation lies in the fact that it can heat substances uniformly. It is believed that microwave irradiation produces a force on charged particles and causes them to move or become further polarized. This leads to friction and collisions of molecules, which subsequently generates heat. Rapid heating by microwave irradiation eliminates the effect of a temperature gradient, contributing to the monodispersion, small size and high purity of the resulting CDots [34–37]. The microwave–hydrothermal method is superior to conventional routes because it takes advantage of the increased reactivity of the precursor and decreases the reaction time through the elevated temperatures and pressures achieved in the reaction vessel. However, the obtained results cannot be explained merely by thermal effects; the so-called “microwave effect” should also be taken into consideration [38].

The mechanism of fluorescence from CDots is not fully understood. It is known that nitric acid breaks down the carbon material into nanosized particles [23]. It also partially oxidizes the CDots to form functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties, which makes CDots hydrophilic and negatively charged [11]. Because of the doping of oxygen into the carbon particles, there must be some emissive energy traps on the surface of the CDots surface that exhibit strong fluorescence [13]. The fluorescent quantum yields of CDots also depend on the doping efficiency of oxygen [11]. The effects of microwave-assisted heating under reflux and the microwave–hydrothermal method, such as thermal effects, nonthermal effects, high temperature and high pressure may introduce more oxygen containing groups effectively, which can improve the quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime. As a result, the CDots synthesized using microwave-assisted techniques possess excellent emission properties, such as strong fluorescence, narrow FWHM, long lifetime and high quantum yield.

Four precursor materials and three synthetic routes have been used to prepare fluorescent CDots. Of the precursors investigated, GO was the most suitable, producing CDots that exhibited the best fluorescent properties. Microwave-assisted routes decreased the reaction time and enhanced the fluorescent properties of CDots because of microwave effects. Of the synthetic routes investigated, the microwave–hydrothermal method is best suited for the preparation of CDots. This reaction took just 30–45 min and the obtained CDots exhibited high quantum yield, long lifetime, narrow emission and distinct absorption.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Key Scientific Program – Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (Nos. 2006CB933100, and 2011CB933600), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. XDJK 2009C079), the Foundation of Southwest University (No. SWNUQ2005007), and the 211 Project of Southwest University (the Third Term) for financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.carbon.2011.03.041.
